Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd maybe listen to some lectures when I felt like it, but there was no real schedule. Sometimes I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly realize something was due and scramble. I know I should have been more organized, but I just couldn't get myself to care enough to set up a proper system.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. Deadlines were pretty much the only thing that got me moving. If nothing was due soon, I probably wasn't studying. I'd have these bursts of activity right before something was due, then go quiet again. It wasn't healthy, but that's just how it went.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or sometimes curiosity when I heard other students talking about something in class. I'd log in to check if I'd missed something important or to see what assignments were coming up. Sometimes I'd get a notification on my phone and that would remind me the LMS existed. It wasn't really a habit, more like a reaction to external pressure.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never felt like a natural part of my day. I tried that gamified task thing they introduced, which was kind of interesting at first, but it didn't stick. I'd forget about it for weeks. Without regular internet access at home, it was harder to build any kind of consistent habit with online stuff.
Q5: The lecture recordings were probably the most useful because I learn better by listening. I could play them while doing other things, which worked for my style. The discussion boards occasionally had good explanations from other students that clicked better than the official materials. But honestly, I didn't explore most of the resources deeply enough to really judge.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the readings and the extra video content. Without internet at home, downloading everything was a hassle, so I'd just skip it. The discussion posts felt pointless to me most of the time—like, why would I write something when I could just read what others said? I only did the bare minimum there.
Q7: If it directly affected my grade and I could see the connection clearly, then it felt worth doing. The gamified tasks were mildly interesting because they had that competitive element, but even that wore off. Mostly, I needed to feel like there was an immediate payoff, otherwise my brain just wouldn't engage with it.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist in my mind. If it wasn't required, I wasn't doing it. Required activities at least had consequences attached, so I'd force myself through them even when I didn't want to. Optional activities felt like extras I couldn't afford to spend energy on, even though I know some of them might have helped.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture audio files when I could get internet access and then listen to them later on my phone. I also had some textbooks and notes from friends that I'd study from. A lot of my actual learning happened offline, just listening to recordings or reviewing printed notes during commutes or at home.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was genuinely studying but the LMS wouldn't know. Like, I'd listen to a downloaded lecture multiple times while walking around, or I'd review my handwritten notes for hours. None of that showed up anywhere online. My exam score was decent partly because of that invisible work.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd download something once, maybe watch or listen to it on the LMS briefly, then continue with it offline for days. I didn't have reliable internet, so that was basically my only option. The LMS probably thought I barely touched those materials, but I actually went through them multiple times.
Q12: There were definitely moments when I just felt scared and overwhelmed. Around midterms, everything piled up and I kind of shut down. I stopped logging in because seeing all the missed assignments and low completion rates just made me feel worse. It was easier to avoid it than face how behind I was.
Q13: Absolutely. I had other responsibilities and activities outside of class that took up mental space. When I was tired or stressed, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. Confusion about what was actually required also made me avoid logging in—I'd rather not deal with it than figure out what I was supposed to do.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd be scrolling through the LMS but not really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere, worried about other stuff. I'd click through videos or skim readings just to feel like I was doing something, but I wasn't actually learning. It was more about easing guilt than genuine engagement.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even important ones. Part of it was fear—I was scared I'd do them wrong or that they'd take forever. So I'd put them off until the last possible moment, which just made everything more stressful. It's a cycle I couldn't seem to break, even knowing it was hurting me.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me use the LMS. Without a deadline looming, I had no urgency. I'd cram everything into the last few hours before something was due. It wasn't a good strategy, but it was the only way I could get myself to actually do anything.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change my behavior much. By the time I got feedback, I'd already moved on mentally or was stressed about the next thing. Sometimes good feedback made me feel briefly better, but it didn't motivate me to engage more consistently. Bad feedback just made me want to avoid the course more.
Q18: The course structure felt kind of loose, which didn't help someone like me who needs more external pressure. If things had been more tightly scheduled with regular check-ins, I might have stayed more on track. The flexibility that probably helped other students just gave me more room to drift away.
Q19: The gamified elements were interesting but not enough to keep me hooked long-term. What made engagement harder was the lack of clear, immediate consequences for falling behind. Also, not having reliable internet made the whole online-focused design feel like it wasn't built for someone in my situation.
Q20: I think sometimes people are trying to figure things out or they're anxious about actually submitting work. You might click around a lot, watch videos, read discussions—but when it comes to putting yourself out there with an assignment, that's scary. Fear of failure can make you active but unproductive.
Q21: That's kind of me, actually. My LMS activity was pretty low, but I still got a decent exam score because I studied offline. I listened to lectures on my phone, reviewed notes, talked to classmates in person. The system doesn't see any of that, but it still counts as real learning and engagement.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked, but they can't show if you actually understood anything. They miss offline studying completely, and they can't measure fear, confusion, or genuine interest. Someone could look super engaged online but be completely lost, or barely show up but actually know the material.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in effort to understand it, not just going through the motions. For me, engagement happened more in those quiet moments listening to lectures on my own than in any LMS activity. It's about mental investment, not just clicks and logins.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I was scared a lot of the time—scared of failing, scared of being behind, scared of not measuring up. It also wouldn't show all the offline studying I did or the conversations with classmates that helped me understand concepts. My real engagement was mostly invisible to the system.
